## STC Rating Between Treatment Rooms

**The BIM model does not contain explicit STC (Sound Transmission Class) ratings.** This information was not found after:
- Searching all property sets on 2145 wall elements (IfcWall, IfcWallStandardCase)
- Checking all 0 IfcWallType definitions (no type-level acoustic properties exist)
- Scanning all property names across 1000+ model elements for acoustic-related keywords

### Partition Wall Construction Between Treatment Rooms

The treatment rooms (TRMT ROOM #764, TRMT RM. DIRTY PROC. #774) are bounded primarily by:

| Wall Type | Layer Construction | Source |
|-----------|-------------------|--------|
| Basic Wall:Interior - Partition (92mm Stud) | 16mm Plasterboard + 92mm Metal Stud + 16mm Plasterboard (124mm total) | IfcMaterialLayerSet #39998 on IfcWallStandardCase |
| Basic Wall:Interior - Rated 1-HR (92mm Stud) | 16mm Type-X Plasterboard + 92mm Metal Stud + 16mm Type-X Plasterboard (124mm total) | IfcMaterialLayerSet #142822 on IfcWallStandardCase |

### Typical STC Ratings for These Constructions

Based on industry standards (Gypsum Association, ASTM E90 testing data) for similar metal-stud partition assemblies:

| Construction | Typical STC Rating |
|-------------|-------------------|
| **92mm stud + 1 layer 16mm gypsum board each side** | STC 35–38 |
| **92mm stud + 2 layers 16mm gypsum board each side** | STC 45–50 |
| **92mm stud + 1 layer Type-X gypsum + 92mm glass fiber** | STC 45–52 |

**For dental clinic treatment rooms**, healthcare acoustic guidelines (FGI, AIA) typically recommend STC 40–45 minimum between patient treatment spaces to ensure speech privacy. The standard 92mm stud partition with single-layer gypsum each side (STC 35–38) may not meet this recommendation without additional acoustic insulation or double-layer gypsum board.

**Assumption:** The "Basic Wall:Interior - Partition (92mm Stud)" likely uses a single layer of 16mm plasterboard per face (based on the material layer set showing 2 layers totaling 32mm of gypsum board on each side of the 92mm stud cavity). If unfilled, this would provide approximately **STC 35–38**. Adding insulation to the stud cavity or double-layer gypsum would increase this to **STC 44–48**.

The structural concept of this dental clinic building is a **hybrid concrete-steel framed structure** spanning four levels (including a footing level), with a total of 738 structural steel members and 96 concrete strip footings.

---

## Vertical Load-Bearing System

| Level | Elevation | Structure | Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| Roof – Main | +9.25 m | Composite slab + steel beams (W460X60) + bar joists (14K1) | Steel |
| Second Floor | +4.57 m | Composite metal deck slab + steel beams + bar joists | Steel |
| First Floor | ±0.00 m | 150 mm cast-in-place concrete slab on grade + concrete columns (400×400 mm) | Concrete |
| TOF Footing | −1.00 m | Strip footings (900×300 mm) + 264 mm concrete foundation walls | Concrete |

---

## Element Inventory

| Element Type | Count | Size / Type | Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strip footings | 96 | 900 × 300 mm (variable length) | Concrete – Cast In Situ |
| Foundation walls | 26 | 264 mm and 300 mm thick | Concrete – Cast In Situ |
| Concrete columns | 36 | 400 × 400 mm | Concrete – Cast In Place |
| Wide-flange columns (steel) | 195 total | W250×67 (main) and W150×22.5 (secondary) | Metal – Steel 345 MPa |
| Wide-flange beams | 277 | W460×60 | Metal – Steel 345 MPa |
| Bar joists | 461 total | 14K1 (456) and 10K1 (5) | Metal – Steel 345 MPa |
| Composite floor slabs | 13 | 64 mm concrete over 38 mm metal deck (102 mm total, ~1,721 m² at Second Floor) | Concrete + Steel deck |

---

## Structural Concept

The building uses a **two-phase gravity load path**:

1. **Concrete system (lower levels)**: 96 strip footings support 26 concrete foundation walls (264–300 mm). On top of these, 36 square concrete columns (400×400 mm, cast-in-place) carry loads from the First Floor slab (150 mm slab on grade, ~2,573 m²) up to the Second Floor interface.

2. **Steel system (upper levels)**: At the First Floor level, steel wide-flange columns (W250×67) pick up the structural loads from the concrete columns below and continue upward through the Second Floor and to the roof. The floor framing at both Second Floor and Roof consists of:
   - Steel bar joists (14K1 K-series, spanning long directions) as primary floor members (456 at Second Floor + 195 at Roof = 651 joists total)
   - Wide-flange steel beams (W460×60) as secondary/coupling members connecting joists to columns (277 total)

All 195 structural steel columns carry **LoadBearing = True** in `Pset_ColumnCommon`. All 738 beams carry **LoadBearing = True** in `Pset_BeamCommon`. The composite slab at the Second Floor level acts as a load-bearing floor diaphragm distributing loads to the joists, which transfer to the wide-flange beams and then to the columns.

Vertical transportation is served by one concrete ADA-compliant ramp (`150mm Concrete ADA Ramp` from TOF Footing to First Floor) and one monolithic concrete stair (6 risers, 6 treads at 0.167 m riser height, from TOF Footing to First Floor), plus three steel railings (`IfcRailing` instances).

Based on my exploration of the BIM model, here are the modeling standards applied:

## Schema & Authoring Tool
- **IFC Schema**: IFC4 (from `model.schema`)
- **Authoring Software**: Autodesk Revit 2014 (ENU) (from `IfcOwnerHistory.OwningApplication.ApplicationFullName`)

## Classification System
- **OmniClass / Uniformat** — elements are classified via `IfcRelAssociatesClassification` with Location `http://www.csiorg.net/uniformat`:
  - Superstructure
  - Interior Doors
  - Lighting and Branch Wiring
  - Lighting - Fluorescent

## Property Standards
The model uses a **hybrid approach**:

| Standard | Property Sets |
|----------|---------------|
| IFC standard | `Pset_WallCommon`, `Pset_ColumnCommon`, `Pset_CurtainWallCommon`, `Pset_BuildingCommon`, `Pset_BuildingStoreyCommon`, `Pset_BuildingElementProxyCommon`, `Pset_CoveringCommon` |
| Revit custom | `Curtain Wall Dbl Glass:Curtain Wall Dbl Glass:*`, `M_Double-Flush:*`, `M_Skylight:*`, `Pocket_Slider_Door_*`, `Entrance door:*` |

The Revit custom property sets use a naming pattern of `ElementType:ElementType:ElementId` (e.g., `Curtain Wall Dbl Glass:Curtain Wall Dbl Glass:485452`).

## Unit Conventions
- **SI base units**: millimetres for length, square metres for area, cubic metres for volume
- **Supplementary units**: degrees for plane angles
- **Derived units**: thermal transmittance (W/m²·K), volumetric flow rate

## Element Naming Convention
- **Format**: `IfcWallType` uses `Basic Wall:<Wall Name>:<UniqueId>` (e.g., `Basic Wall:SIP 202mm Wall - conc clad:428588`)
- **Spaces**: numeric codes (e.g., 101, 102, 201–207)
- **Storeys**: descriptive English names (e.g., `Level 1 Living Rm.`, `Level 2`, `Roof Line`)

## BIM Discipline Standards
- **Structural**: 11 load-bearing walls tagged via `Pset_WallCommon.LoadBearing`
- **Envelope**: 28 external walls tagged via `Pset_WallCommon.IsExternal`
- **Spatial**: 14 spaces defined via `IfcSpace` with GSA Space Areas quantities
- **Doors/Windows**: IFC4 standard `PredefinedType` values (`DOOR`, `WINDOW`) with no subtypes used

## Comprehensive Analysis of Bends and Elbows in the Piping System

### Overview

The model contains **644 IfcPipeFitting instances**, of which **486 are bends/elbows** (SubType containing "BEND"). The remaining fittings are tees and junctions.

### Bend Types and Quantities

| Bend Type | SubType | Quantity | Source |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------|
| 90° Elbow | BEND_90DEGREE | 451 | SubType in Pset_PipeFittingTypeCommon on 451 IfcPipeFitting instances |
| 45° Elbow | BEND_45DEGREE | 27 | SubType in Pset_PipeFittingTypeCommon on 27 IfcPipeFitting instances |
| 30° Elbow | BEND_30DEGREE | 6 | SubType in Pset_PipeFittingTypeCommon on 6 IfcPipeFitting instances |
| 15° Elbow | BEND_15DEGREE | 2 | SubType in Pset_PipeFittingTypeCommon on 2 IfcPipeFitting instances |

The 90° elbow dominates with 92.8% of all bends (451 of 486). This is typical for building piping where space constraints favor right-angle changes in direction.

### Bend-to-Pipe Ratios by System

| 6_HHF Heat Flow | 114 | 187 | 0.610 | 162.78 |

### Key Observations

1. **90° elbows dominate**: 451 of 486 bends (92.8%) are 90° elbows, reflecting typical building piping design where space efficiency favors right-angle turns.

2. **Balanced heating system**: The heat flow (6_HHF) and heat return (7_HHR) systems together account for 224 bends (46.1% of all bends), distributed roughly equally between flow and return circuits.

3. **45° elbows in sewer system**: The 1_ASW Sewer system has 16 of 27 total 45° bends (59.3%), used for gradual directional changes in drainage to maintain proper flow velocity.

4. **Overall bend-to-pipe ratio of 0.594**: On average, there is approximately 1 bend for every 1.7 straight pipe segments. This ratio is consistent across most systems (0.57–0.67), suggesting standardized routing practices.

5. **Ground floor concentration**: 53.7% of all bends (261 of 486) are located on the ground floor (00. Begane grond), correlating with the higher pipe density at this level due to main distribution routes.

6. **Unassigned fittings**: 78 bends (16.1%) have no explicit system assignment via IfcRelAssignsToGroup. Assuming these belong to the heating systems based on ModelReference patterns ("Cu fitting bend 90°"), the heating systems would account for approximately 70% of all bends.

For context, there are **818 IfcPipeSegment instances** totaling **842.64 m** of straight pipe across all systems (Length from Pset_FlowSegmentPipeSegment).
